findings.description: Nested <main> landmark elements - ensure the document has at most one main landmark at top level
findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 32. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: Table A1 is split across multiple pages in the PDF with a clear continuation, but in the HTML the table appears to have a visual break/gap between K24 and K25 rows, suggesting a table split that creates two separate visual table sections rather than one continuous table.
findings.evidence: PDF shows Table A1 as one continuous table across pages. HTML shows a visible gap/break between K24 and K25 rows, and another between K51 and K52, creating the appearance of separate table segments.

findings.description: Table A2 in the PDF is presented as a single continuous table of emission rates. In the HTML, the table appears to be broken into multiple separate table segments with gaps between them (K1-K10, then K11-K38, then K39-K62 plus ENVRTO/ENVRCO).
findings.evidence: PDF shows Table A2 as one continuous table. HTML renders it as multiple disconnected table segments with visible breaks.

findings.description: Table A1 contains spurious rows with embedded images that break the table structure. Between K24-K25 and K51-K52, there are extra table rows containing only image references in the first cell and empty cells for the remaining columns. These are artifacts of page-by-page conversion and should not appear as data rows within the table.
findings.evidence: HTML contains rows like: ... inserted between K24 and K25, and between K51 and K52. Similarly in Table A2, image rows appear between K10 and K11, and between K38 and K39.

findings.description: The document has nested <main> elements. The header content (DIVISION OF AIR QUALITY, date) is placed in a <header> inside the first <main>, and then a second <main> element contains the rest. This is invalid HTML structure, though not a heading hierarchy issue per se.
